title: Verification-gated harnesses let coding agents self-improve a real lab overnight
summary: Nvidia's ENPIRE and OpenAI's AI chemist show the same pattern: a harness wraps coding agents with reset, verification, and a keep-only-if-it-helps gate so they improve real-world tasks unattended.
takeaways: Wrap an autonomous agent in a harness that auto-resets the task, verifies each result, and keeps a change only when it raises the measured success rate — the loop, not the prompt, is what makes self-improvement safe to leave running.

The same four-module shape (reset/verify, refine, parallel eval, failure analysis from logs and papers) carried across three different coding agents, so the harness is the durable asset, not any one model.
Even the most autonomous published loops keep a human in the gate — selecting which proposals to physically test and running validation by hand.

sources.title: Ars Technica: AI coding agents direct robot training (Nvidia GEAR ENPIRE)
OpenAI: A near-autonomous AI chemist improves a Chan-Lam reaction

body:
## What happened

On 2026-06-17, [Ars Technica reported](https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/06/ai-coding-agents-can-autonomously-direct-robot-training/) that Nvidia's GEAR (Generalist Embodied Agent Research) lab, with Carnegie Mellon and UC Berkeley, built an agent harness — software that wraps a model with memory, tools, and feedback loops — called ENPIRE that lets coding agents autonomously direct training of physical robots, teaching them to cut zip ties and insert GPUs into motherboard sockets. Director of AI Jim Fan said "a part of our NVIDIA GEAR lab now self-improves tirelessly overnight. We just read the reports in the morning." The same day, [OpenAI described](https://openai.com/index/ai-chemist-improves-reaction) a near-autonomous AI chemist: GPT-5.4 wired into Molecule.one's "Maria" ran 10,080 reactions over two cycles and lifted mean yield on a drug-making reaction from 16.6% to 25.2%.

## Why it matters

Leaving an agent to run unattended is where most teams stop, because a wrong autonomous change is silently retained and compounds. What lets both labs leave the loop running overnight is not a smarter prompt but the harness around the model: it decides what counts as progress and refuses to keep anything that doesn't. That gate is the engineering work.

## How it works

1. **Reset and verify.** ENPIRE's first module automatically resets each task and verifies the outcome, so every trial starts from a known state and produces a trustworthy success signal.
2. **Refine the policy.** A second module proposes changes to the policy that guides robot behavior, the way the AI chemist proposed using a mild oxidant (TEMPO) as an additive.
3. **Evaluate in parallel.** Candidates run across multiple physical robots at once, cutting the wall-clock cost of testing.
4. **Analyze failures.** A module reads logs and ingests research papers to diagnose failures and rewrite the training code.
5. **Keep only what helps.** The agents retain a change only when it raises the measured success rate over repeated self-directed cycles.

> The harness keeps a change only when verification says it raised the measured success rate.

## What broke

The autonomy is bounded by design, and that is the lesson. [OpenAI](https://openai.com/index/ai-chemist-improves-reaction) calls its three-month workflow only "near-autonomous": humans wrote prompts, selected which proposals to test, corrected experimental plans, and ran validation by hand — chemists confirmed 11 of 14 substrate pairs at the bench. OpenAI cautions the result is early and does not show end-to-end autonomous research. The fix is harness engineering, not a freer agent: keep verification and human selection in the gate so a self-running loop can't bank a bad result. That [the same ENPIRE harness](https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/06/ai-coding-agents-can-autonomously-direct-robot-training/) carried across OpenAI's Codex, Anthropic's Claude Code, and Moonshot's Kimi Code shows the harness, not the model, is the durable asset.

[Harness Engineering](/guide/harness-engineering/)
